intro.speaker: Cipher
lines: I'm sending you deep into the PULSE settings, where ALGO keeps the rules deliberately confusing. A shiny new feature called PULSE Connect promises an easier login and 'a more personal experience'.

The catch is buried in the consent screen: a pre-ticked box, wording built to confuse you, and a bright 'Accept all' button next to a grey, hidden 'Manage settings'. These are dark patterns. Read the small print for one thing only: who gets your data, and whether you can say no.

learningGoals.media: Die Schüler können manipulative Gestaltungstricks in Einwilligungsdialogen ('dark patterns', Voreinstellungen, irreführende Buttons) erkennen und Privatsphäre-Einstellungen bewusst und datensparsam wählen.
learningGoals.english: Die Schüler können englische Nutzungsbedingungen und Datenschutztexte im Detail verstehen, wichtige Klauseln herausfiltern und Fachvokabular (consent, permission, third party, opt out, data sharing) korrekt zuordnen.

puzzles.prompt: Here is the PULSE Connect consent screen. It shows an already-ticked consent box near the top left, a huge glowing green 'Accept all' button in the centre, a tiny grey 'Manage settings' link tucked in the bottom corner, a bold red fear line reading 'You may miss out on friends', and a plain, honest link to the privacy policy. Draw a box around every dark pattern ALGO is using to steer you towards 'Accept all'. Leave the honest privacy-policy link alone.

regions.label: Fear line 'You may miss out on friends'

feedback.correct: Spotted. Every trick pulls you toward 'Accept all' without thinking. The plain privacy-policy link is the only honest thing on the screen.
feedback.wrong: Look again. Anything that nudges, hides or frightens is a dark pattern. A plain, neutral link is not.

hints: The easy, colourful button is the one ALGO wants you to press. Look for the quiet options around it.
Dark patterns nudge, hide or frighten. A box already ticked for you, a link made hard to see, and a warning that you'll 'miss out' are all tricks.
Tap the pre-ticked box, the glowing 'Accept all' button, the grey 'Manage settings' link and the 'You may miss out' fear line. Leave the plain privacy-policy link untapped.

puzzles.prompt: Read this extract from the PULSE Connect terms: 'By continuing, you allow PULSE and its third-party partners to access and share your location and contacts. You may opt out of this sharing at any time through Manage settings.' Which statement is correct?
options: Only PULSE itself sees your location and contacts, and sharing cannot be stopped.
Location and contacts go to third-party partners, but you can opt out through Manage settings.
Nothing is shared with anyone unless you press 'Accept all'.
Your data is shared and there is no way to say no.

feedback.correct: Exactly. Your location and contacts reach third-party partners, but you can opt out, just not through the button ALGO wants you to press.
feedback.wrong: Read the clause once more. It names third-party partners and it names a way to opt out.

hints: Read the terms for one thing only: who gets your data, and whether you can say no.
The phrase 'third-party partners' tells you the data leaves PULSE. The phrase 'opt out' tells you refusal is possible.
The correct answer is that location and contacts go to third-party partners, and you can opt out, but only through 'Manage settings', not 'Accept all'.

puzzles.prompt: This is the PULSE permission panel. A well-behaved app should ask only for what a feature genuinely needs. Select every permission you should DENY because the app does not truly need it to work.
options: Constant microphone access in the background
Photos, only when you actively upload one
Location, only while using the app

feedback.correct: That's data-sparing done right. Deny constant microphone, the contacts upload and ad-tracking. Allow photos on active upload and location only while using.
feedback.wrong: Ask of each switch: does the app truly need this to work? If not, deny it. Photos-on-upload and location-while-using are fine.

hints: Grant a permission only if the feature would break without it.
An app does not need your microphone running all the time, your entire contact list, or ad-tracking just to let you post.
Deny the constant microphone, the contacts-list upload and the ad-tracking. Leave photos-on-upload and location-while-using allowed.

puzzles.prompt: Final code system. You denied exactly three switches (microphone, contacts, ad-tracking), and the terms told you refusal is possible using a two-word phrase. Combine the number of switches you set to 'deny' with that key phrase from the terms. Type the release code in the form DENY-<number>-<PHRASE>.

feedback.correct: Code accepted. Three switches denied, and you can 'opt out'. The mission closes on your terms, not ALGO's.
feedback.wrong: Count your denied switches, then add the two-word phrase from the terms that means 'say no'.

hints: The code starts with DENY, then a number, then a keyword from the terms.
You denied three permissions. The terms said you 'may ... at any time' do one particular thing to refuse.
The code is DENY-3-OPTOUT: three switches on deny plus the phrase 'opt out'.

outro.speaker: Cipher
lines: 'Easy' was the trap. He pre-ticks the boxes so you agree without noticing.
You don't have to hide from everything, just choose on purpose. Deny what the app doesn't actually need. Nicely done, Agent.
